mission.title: Install Open WebUI on Kubernetes
mission.description: Open WebUI (formerly Ollama WebUI) is the popular self-hosted AI chat frontend from open-webui.com. It is a Python and SvelteKit app that talks to any OpenAI-compatible backend — Ollama, vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LiteLLM, RHAIIS — and adds user accounts, chat history, model switching, RAG, and tool calls on top. This mission installs Open WebUI via the official Helm chart at https://helm.openwebui.com/ , wires it to a backend Service in the same cluster, and exposes the web UI through a ClusterIP Service that you can port-forward.

steps.title: Decide on a backend LLM runner
steps.description:
Open WebUI itself does not run inference — it is a frontend. Pick (or install) one of:

- **Ollama** — simplest, single-binary. Install via the `install-ollama` mission if you do not already have one.
- **vLLM** — GPU-accelerated production inference. See `install-vllm`.
- **LocalAI** — CPU-first OpenAI-compatible runtime. See `install-localai`.
- **llama.cpp** — lightest weight, CPU or CUDA. See `install-llama-cpp`.
- **Red Hat AI Inference Server** — OpenShift-native, Red Hat-supported. See `install-rhaiis`.

You will point Open WebUI at the backend's ClusterIP Service URL in the next step. Confirm the backend is already running:
```bash
kubectl get svc -A | grep -Ei 'ollama|vllm|local-ai|llama-server|rhaiis'
```

steps.title: Add the Open WebUI Helm repository
steps.description:
Open WebUI publishes charts at https://helm.openwebui.com/ and the chart is named `open-webui`:
```bash
helm repo add open-webui https://helm.openwebui.com/ 
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Create the namespace
steps.description:
Keep Open WebUI in its own namespace for easy cleanup:
```bash
kubectl create namespace open-webui
```

steps.title: Prepare a values file
steps.description:
Write a small values override pointing at your backend. The example below targets an in-cluster LocalAI Service running at `http://local-ai.localai.svc.cluster.local:8080/v1` — replace with your actual backend URL. `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL` is the Open WebUI env var that accepts any OpenAI-compatible endpoint:
```bash
cat > openwebui-values.yaml <<'EOF'
replicaCount: 1
image:
  repository: ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui
  tag: main
ollama:
  enabled: false
extraEnvVars:
  - name: OPENAI_API_BASE_URL
    value: http://local-ai.localai.svc.cluster.local:8080/v1
  - name: OPENAI_API_KEY
    value: sk-not-required-for-local-backends
  - name: ENABLE_SIGNUP
    value: "true"
  - name: WEBUI_AUTH
    value: "true"
persistence:
  enabled: true
  size: 5Gi
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
service:
  type: ClusterIP
  port: 8080
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: 1Gi
  limits:
    cpu: "2"
    memory: 4Gi
EOF
```
For Ollama as backend, set `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL` to `http://<ollama-svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:11434/v1` . For vLLM, set it to `http://<vllm-svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1` . Leave `WEBUI_AUTH: true` so the first user-created account becomes the admin — disable only for demo clusters, never production.

steps.title: Install the chart
steps.description:
Install Open WebUI into the `open-webui` namespace and wait for the Deployment to roll out. The first start takes about a minute because Open WebUI initializes its SQLite database on the PVC:
```bash
helm install open-webui open-webui/open-webui -n open-webui -f openwebui-values.yaml
kubectl -n open-webui rollout status deploy/open-webui --timeout=5m
```

steps.title: Access the web UI
steps.description:
Port-forward the Service to your workstation and open http://127.0.0.1:8080/ in a browser:
```bash
kubectl -n open-webui port-forward svc/open-webui 8080:8080
```
The first user to sign up becomes the admin. From the admin settings, the backend you wired via `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL` should already be visible under Connections, and the models served by that backend should appear in the model picker.

resolution.summary: A successful Open WebUI install has the `open-webui` Deployment rolled out in the `open-webui` namespace, the persistence PVC bound, and the web UI at http://127.0.0.1:8080/ (via port-forward) showing a sign-in page. After creating the admin account, the backend Connection resolves and the model picker lists at least one model served by the backend.
codeSnippets: helm install open-webui open-webui/open-webui -n open-webui -f openwebui-values.yaml

uninstall.title: Delete the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Removing the release tears down the Deployment, Service, and ConfigMaps:
```bash
helm uninstall open-webui -n open-webui
```

uninstall.title: Delete the persistence PVC
uninstall.description:
Helm does not delete PVCs on uninstall. The Open WebUI PVC holds user accounts, chat history, and RAG uploads. If you want to fully reset the install, delete it explicitly:
```bash
kubectl -n open-webui delete pvc -l app.kubernetes.io/name=open-webui --ignore-not-found
```

uninstall.title: Delete the namespace
uninstall.description:
Finally remove the namespace:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace open-webui --ignore-not-found
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repo
upgrade.description:
Open WebUI publishes chart updates alongside every release. Refresh your local cache before upgrading:
```bash
helm repo update open-webui
helm search repo open-webui/open-webui --versions
```

upgrade.title: Roll out the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Open WebUI performs its own SQLite schema migration on start. Use `helm upgrade` with the same values file — the PVC is preserved so user accounts and chats survive the upgrade:
```bash
helm upgrade open-webui open-webui/open-webui -n open-webui -f openwebui-values.yaml
kubectl -n open-webui rollout status deploy/open-webui --timeout=5m
```

troubleshooting.title: Sign-in page loads but model picker is empty
troubleshooting.description:
If the UI loads but no models appear, Open WebUI could not reach the backend. Check the pod logs for `OPENAI` or `connection refused`:
```bash
kubectl -n open-webui logs deploy/open-webui --tail=100
```
The most common causes are (a) a typo in `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL`, (b) the backend Service is in a different namespace than the DNS name suggests, or (c) a NetworkPolicy blocking egress from the open-webui namespace to the backend namespace. Confirm the backend is reachable from an ephemeral debug pod:
```bash
kubectl run curl-test --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl --restart=Never -- curl -v http://local-ai.localai.svc.cluster.local:8080/v1/models
```

troubleshooting.title: First admin account cannot sign up
troubleshooting.description: If sign-up fails with `Signups are disabled`, the `ENABLE_SIGNUP` env var was not set. Either patch the Deployment to add it and restart, or use the Open WebUI CLI helper to create an admin user by exec-ing into the pod. See https://docs.openwebui.com/ for the helper command.

troubleshooting.title: PVC stuck Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If `kubectl rollout status` times out and the pod is stuck, the persistence PVC may not have bound. Check:
```bash
kubectl -n open-webui get pvc
kubectl -n open-webui describe pvc
```
The usual cause is a cluster with no default StorageClass. Either set a default (`kubectl patch storageclass <name> -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"true"}}}'`) or set `persistence.storageClass` in the values file.

troubleshooting.title: Pod OOMKilled on a large RAG upload
troubleshooting.description: Open WebUI runs embedding and chunking for RAG uploads in the same pod by default. Large PDFs or document uploads can push memory past the container limit. Raise `resources.limits.memory` to 4-8 GiB in the values file and redeploy.

security.title: WEBUI_AUTH must stay true
security.description: Open WebUI ships with an authentication flag that can be disabled for demo installs (`WEBUI_AUTH=false`). Never run with authentication disabled in a shared environment — any client that reaches the Service becomes the anonymous admin. Keep `WEBUI_AUTH=true` and create your admin account as the first sign-up, then disable `ENABLE_SIGNUP` to lock out additional accounts.

security.title: Secrets in values files
security.description:
If your backend requires an API key (for instance, pointing at a hosted OpenRouter or Anthropic endpoint), do not hardcode the key in `openwebui-values.yaml`. Instead, create a Kubernetes Secret and reference it via `envFrom`:
```bash
kubectl -n open-webui create secret generic open-webui-env --from-literal=OPENAI_API_KEY=<your-key>
```
Then add `envFrom.[].secretRef.name: open-webui-env` to the values file. For local-only backends, the API key is unused and `sk-not-required-for-local-backends` is a reasonable placeholder that never leaves the cluster.

security.title: No hostPath or privileged access required
security.description: The Open WebUI chart runs as an unprivileged container with no hostPath mounts, no hostNetwork, and no CAP_SYS_ADMIN. It should pass the Kubernetes Pod Security Admission `baseline` profile as-is. For `restricted` enforcement, set `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true`, `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, `seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault`, and drop all Linux capabilities in your values override.

security.title: Egress surface
security.description: Open WebUI makes outbound calls to (a) the backend LLM endpoint you configured, (b) the OpenAI API for model listings if `OPENAI_API_BASE_URL` points at openai.com, and (c) on first start, github.com for release-check telemetry. If your cluster enforces default-deny egress via NetworkPolicy, allow traffic to the backend namespace and deny everything else. Disable release-check telemetry by setting `SAFE_MODE=true` in the env vars.

security.title: Relationship to the KubeStellar Console SECURITY-MODEL
security.description: Open WebUI is a chat frontend — it can send user chat content to whatever backend you point it at. For the strongest posture, point Open WebUI at an in-cluster backend (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, Ollama) and use NetworkPolicy to deny egress from the open-webui namespace to the public internet. See docs/security/SECURITY-MODEL.md in the kubestellar/console repo for the Console's own architecture around chat-content handling and kubeconfig isolation.

prerequisites.description: Kubernetes 1.24+ with a default StorageClass that supports ReadWriteOnce volumes, Helm 3, and a reachable OpenAI-compatible backend already running in the cluster (Ollama, vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, or RHAIIS). Outbound egress to ghcr.io is required on first pull for the Open WebUI image.
